title: Promoting avian diversity in cities requires multiple parks with variable features

data_scope.note: Exact eBird sampling effort, dataset versions and calendar years are not stated in the available metadata

core_conclusion: No single park design maximizes urban bird biodiversity. Across 935 parks in 186 US cities, species richness rises with park area and water, phylogenetic diversity is higher near the urban boundary, and functional diversity is higher where habitat heterogeneity is lower. The effect of tree canopy cover flips sign geographically (beneficial in the Intermountain West, negative in eastern cities for richness and phylogenetic diversity). Conserving all facets requires a portfolio of multiple parks with variable features, tuned regionally and seasonally.
why_it_matters: Splits biodiversity into three conflicting facets to show single-metric green planning fails; provides a portfolio rule (diversify parks, not perfect one park); reveals tree canopy cover reverses sign by region and season; built on continental eBird data across 186 cities.
why_nature: Continental-scale transferable findings across US biogeographic regions; couples a multi-facet biodiversity framework to actionable, regionally specific park-design guidance; counterintuitive sign-flip result; frames parks as multi-species infrastructure.
added_value_to_my_research: Geographically weighted SHAP / MGWR to map the tree-canopy sign-flip as local coefficients
Three-objective park portfolio = facility-location optimization reusing my efficiency-equity toolchain
2SFCA/R2SFCA nature-access layer weighting parks by their three diversity facets
Spatiotemporal + legacy/migration modeling of park diversity over time
Physics/ecology-informed GNN as a transferable urban-biodiversity predictor with sampling debiasing
